aliases: Braised Kelp Tofu Mustard Vegetable Platter

english: Braised Kelp Tofu Mustard Vegetable Platter

region_of_origin: China (Beijing Hutong tradition), served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Chinese cold appetiser platter / assorted small dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Chinese 5-dish cold platter. FOODON_03315116 (mixed dish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Mixed dishes) is the closest available category. No specific code exists for a Chinese multi-component cold platter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific multi-component Chinese appetiser platter.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of five small dishes, estimated at approximately 400 g total, typically shared among 2–4 people as a starter or side.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Seaweed (kelp), Tofu (firm), Mustard greens (cooked), Mixed vegetables (stir-fried), Hawthorn fruit
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific 5-dish platter. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of the five component foods using USDA FoodData Central data: braised kelp (~20% weight), mashed tofu (~25% weight), mustard-dipped vegetables (~20% weight), sauteed assorted vegetables (~20% weight), crystal red fruits/hawthorn (~15% weight). Proportions are approximate based on typical Chinese cold platter serving conventions. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a plant-based, lightly seasoned mixed platter.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and tofu-based dishes; analogous estimate based on tofu (GI ~15), cooked vegetables (GI ~30–40), seaweed (GI ~15), and fruit components (GI ~40–55) — weighted composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific 5-dish platter. GI estimated as a weighted composite of component foods using University of Sydney GI Database reference values: firm tofu GI ~15 (25% weight), braised kelp GI ~15 (20% weight), mustard-dipped vegetables GI ~32 (20% weight), sauteed mixed vegetables GI ~38 (20% weight), hawthorn/crystal red fruit GI ~50 (15% weight). Weighted composite GI ≈ 32, classified as low. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (32 × 8.2 × 4.0) / 100 ≈ 10.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from braising liquid and mustard seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is estimated at approximately 280 mg per 100 g (1,120 mg per 400 g platter), which approaches the daily limit of 1,500–2,300 mg recommended for hypertensive individuals; those with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to one or two dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high fibre, tofu protein, and seaweed content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and glycemic load of approximately 10.5 per shared platter, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the tofu and kelp components provide protein and soluble fibre that further attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Plant-based composition, low saturated fat, and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The platter is entirely plant-based with negligible saturated fat (~0.3 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly choice; however, the sodium content from seasoning should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Flavour of Hutong 5-Dish Platter (胡同味道 5拼) is a Chinese cold appetiser assortment served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局), a Beijing-style Chinese restaurant in Singapore. The platter draws inspiration from the hutong (胡同) alleyway food culture of old Beijing, where small, flavourful cold dishes are served as starters or accompaniments to a main meal. The five components are: (1) Braised Kelp — tender strips of seaweed slow-braised in a savoury soy-based sauce, rich in iodine and dietary fibre; (2) Traditional Mashed Tofu — silken or firm tofu mashed and seasoned with sesame oil, garlic, and light soy, providing plant-based protein and calcium; (3) Mustard Dipped — blanched vegetables served with a pungent Chinese mustard dressing, adding a sharp, sinus-clearing heat; (4) Sauteed Assorted Vegetables — a lightly stir-fried medley of seasonal vegetables seasoned with garlic and minimal oil; (5) Crystal Red Fruits — likely hawthorn berries or a similar sweet-tart preserved fruit, providing a palate-cleansing finish. The overall nutritional profile is plant-based, low in saturated fat, moderate in sodium, and low on the glycemic index (estimated GI ~32). The platter is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is broadly appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, with sodium monitoring advised for hypertensive individuals. Key nutrients include dietary fibre (2.4 g/100 g), plant protein (3.8 g/100 g), calcium from tofu and kelp, and iodine from seaweed.
common_user_queries: Is the Hutong platter suitable for diabetics?
What dishes are in the 胡同味道 5拼?
How many calories in the Hutong 5-dish platter?
Is the Hutong platter high in sodium?
What is crystal red fruit in Chinese food?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA) for this specific 5-dish platter as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component-food data using approximate weight proportions. GI is an analogous weighted estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for individual components. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component foods; no single approved-source entry covers this exact platter. 2. Component weight proportions (kelp 20%, tofu 25%, mustard veg 20%, sauteed veg 20%, crystal fruit 15%) are approximate and based on typical Chinese cold platter conventions. 3. Sodium estimate (280 mg/100 g) reflects light braising and seasoning; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on restaurant preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is an all-plant dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA component data. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all components are plant-based. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for this plant-based cold platter; kelp contains negligible vitamin D. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. 9. GI estimated at 32 (low) using weighted composite of University of Sydney GI Database reference values for tofu, kelp, vegetables, and hawthorn fruit. 10. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a shared platter; actual restaurant portion may differ. 11. Allergens include soy (tofu) and mustard; individuals with soy or mustard allergies should avoid this dish. 12. The dish name references hutong (胡同) alleyway culture of Beijing, China.
